title: TBM 389: Overthinker!
questions: How do I balance reductionist clarity with systems complexity without betraying reality or my identity?
When does “overthinking” become a dismissal, and how can I respond constructively at work?
What does it mean to “win” in organizational or social change—being right in principle or being useful in practice?
How can we design and test change through prototypes instead of only modeling or diagnosing?
search_terms: two truths doctrine Nagargjuna conventional ultimate truth
finger pointing at the moon meaning
summary: This post explores how “overthinking” and reductionist thinking show up in workplaces and leadership, often as a way to dismiss complexity or control others. It argues for a third way that treats reduction and complexity as lenses, then turns insight into useful action through engagement, ownership, and prototyping. It’s aimed at people who feel boxed in by simplifiers and want a practical, identity-aligned approach to navigating organizational change.

title: TBM 380: “But We Have To Try Something!”
questions: Why do organizations keep returning to the same outcomes even after big change initiatives?
How can leaders communicate systems or complexity insights without triggering narrative fallacy?
What does it mean to co-author the narrative around change, and how do you do it in practice?
How can teams make risks visible earlier and commitments more reliable without scapegoating or simplistic fixes?
search_terms: work in progress reduce WIP narrative
summary: The post argues that organizational change often fails because leaders and teams default to narrative fallacies—neat stories that feel coherent but don’t alter the underlying attractor states. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners who want to apply systems/complexity thinking while still providing actionable plans and a compelling action-to-outcome narrative. The example shows reframing “predictability” into practices that make risks visible earlier and commitments more reliable, creating space for complexity-aware experimentation.

title: TBM 362: How (And Why) We Help
questions: When does “helping” through a consulting playbook hurt the helper or others?
Is it possible for an outcome to be wrong even if it satisfies the majority’s needs?
Should you work within a system’s dominant frame or challenge it when you believe another model is better?
What do we owe clients or colleagues: expertise and direction, or time, space, and trust to find their own way?
search_terms: helping that harms self and others
summary: This post contrasts a dominant gap-and-expert consulting model with a relational, capacity-building approach (inspired by Peter Block) and explores how each shapes context, authority, and change. It’s for change agents, product/ops leaders, and advisors who feel tension between “installed” transformation narratives and the slower work of shared ownership—especially when incentives and power dynamics make helping feel harmful. The post argues that naming the stance you take (“which hat you wear”) can increase self-awareness and honesty about how you show up with others.

title: TBM 342: Who Pays the Ego Toll?
questions: Who pays the ego toll when we try to implement an easy, obvious improvement?
What status, authority, or credibility would be lost if we changed our way of working?
How can we reframe change as specific and temporary to lower threat to identity?
How can we shift status and rewards so people feel respected while adopting a new collaboration model?
search_terms: status shift instead of removing status
summary: This post explains why teams often fail to act on obvious, low-hanging improvements: the real barrier is the “ego toll” paid by individuals and groups who must risk credibility, authority, and identity. It’s aimed at leaders and team members trying to drive collaboration changes (e.g., direct cross-functional work) without triggering destabilizing resistance. The piece offers practical prompts for reframing change to preserve identity, reduce threat, and realign status and incentives.

title: systems *doing* and complexity *doing*
questions: How do we run experiments in complex environments without turning them into excuses for unilateral action?
What does it mean to “do by not doing” when we feel pressure to react immediately?
How can teams hold competing narratives and avoid premature synthesis or narrative fallacy?
When should we resist breaking a system into parts and instead work with the whole as an interacting context?
search_terms: constraint as a tool for discussion
summary: The post outlines practical behaviors for working in complex systems, emphasizing how to facilitate collaboration across perspectives while sustaining ambiguity. It’s aimed at leaders and teams who need to run experiments, avoid premature convergence, and balance simplification with fidelity to messy underlying realities.

title: TBM 303: Official, Real, and the Ideal
questions: How does my company balance official processes, real day-to-day work, and the effort to translate between them?
Where do we engage in compliance theater or box-checking that fails to help frontline teams?
When we claim things are “fine” or “just work,” are we describing reality or selling an optics-driven ideal?
How can we spread local team best practices without denying teams’ reality or creating new heavy formal dependencies?
summary: The post proposes a five-part lens for understanding how organizations juggle official processes, real work, the translation effort between them, optics-driven idealizations, and well-meaning improvement directions. It’s aimed at leaders and managers who want to diagnose why “everything is fine” narratives persist, especially around compliance, dashboards, and dependency-heavy processes. It also warns that genuine change can be mistaken for—or turn into—another form of idealization that denies teams’ lived reality.

title: 20 Things I've Learned as a Systems (Over) Thinker (Extended Commentary)
questions: How do I communicate complex systems insights without overwhelming or threatening others?
Who should I do deep thinking with—how do I choose co-conspirators vs people I’m trying to influence?
How can I influence change when formal authority isn’t available?
What should I expect from change efforts over time if progress is discontinuous?
search_terms: how to communicate complex ideas simply
summary: This post offers a set of practical lessons for people who naturally think deeply about systems and complex problems, especially when trying to drive change in organizations. It focuses on managing cognitive load and recovery, communicating insights without overwhelming others, and navigating influence, power structures, and the slow/discontinuous nature of progress. It’s aimed at systems thinkers, change agents, and informal leaders who want to be effective without burning out or overstepping.

title: TBM 286: Narrative Fallacies and the 2024 Narrative Soup
questions: Why do corporate explanations for inefficiency feel compelling but turn out to be wrong?
What causes 'narrative soup' and narrative camps inside large organizations?
How do layoffs and asymmetric pressures affect unity of purpose and corporate transformation stories?
What should an individual do when public narratives become their internal narratives?
search_terms: why corporate transformation stories feel like myths
narratives camps and identity at work
time wasted waiting on salaried people
fragile explanations when confronted with reality
summary: The post argues that people explain organizational inefficiency through narrative fallacies—emotionally satisfying stories that can become identity anchors and fuel conflict. It is aimed at leaders and knowledge workers trying to make sense of corporate transformation and discourse in 2024’s divisive environment, where clashing stakeholder narratives create a 'narrative soup.' The recommended response is self-reflection and safer ways to seek support and diverse perspectives.

title: TBM 273: Transition and Reflection
questions: How do we move beyond the system vs individual blame/credit loop in workplace problems?
What role do belief system clashes play in team and organizational conflict?
How can we create coherence across strategy, incentives, architecture, culture, and goals?
Should capability models come after alignment and awareness work—or can they lead?
summary: This post is a personal reflection on how to think about work, improvement, and collaboration, especially after experiencing a RIF and transitioning roles. It argues that progress in organizations depends on moving beyond the system/individual dichotomy, building self/other awareness around belief-system tensions, and seeking coherence across multiple “frames” before using the capability view to drive change. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners who want a pragmatic, learning-oriented approach to organizational improvement.

title: TBM 253: Don't Fix Things. Write a New Story
questions: Why does aligning on the problem definition backfire in organizations?
How do competing narratives create factional conflict and oversimplification of complex issues?
What can leaders do to break a narrative stalemate without relying on an external existential threat?
How can a positive organizational narrative help people row in the same direction?
search_terms: sociotechnical systems no single root cause
how to craft a shared story at work
stop fixing things write a new story
summary: The post argues that organizational “problem alignment” is often counterproductive because people interpret what’s broken through different narratives shaped by their worldview and incentives. It explains how competing authority-backed narratives can produce a narrative stalemate where conflict replaces problem solving, and proposes that leaders should craft a new, positive story that enables shared action. The guidance is aimed at leaders and change agents trying to move teams forward amid persistent disagreement.

title: TBM 226: Stuck In The Valley of "Doing OK"
questions: Why do companies drift from “doing OK” into obscurity instead of improving?
How can leaders and teams create meaningful change when the situation is not obviously broken?
What should you do when improvement efforts threaten core strengths and trigger risk aversion?
How do you disrupt a system safely without triggering a threat response?
search_terms: seeding new behaviors to create new attractors
summary: This post explains why organizations can become stuck in a “doing OK” plateau that slowly drifts toward obscurity, using dynamic-systems ideas like basins of attraction. It’s aimed at leaders and team members trying to drive change in environments where problems are on the radar but decisive action feels risky or unnecessary. The author argues for safe disruption—seeding behaviors, increasing connectivity, adjusting incentives, and timing localized actions—rather than complacency accusations or superficial reorgs.

title: TBM 203: Fixing Messy Problems
questions: How can we tell whether a problem is complex or merely complicated?
What makes an experiment a “good” one versus a “bad” one in complex systems?
How do we persuade leaders and teams that a complex problem can still be improved?
How do we navigate change when people’s identities and power are tied to the current root-cause story?
search_terms: why rigid frameworks fail in complex systems
summary: This post is an accessible entry point into how to approach messy, complex organizational problems without pretending there is a single root cause. It argues that progress comes from selecting and running experiments (including leverage-point interventions) while treating communication, humility, and people’s identity/power dynamics as part of the system. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners who feel stuck in analysis paralysis or repeated failed “fixes,” and want a practical way to improve without overconfidence.

title: TBM 9/52: Writing Culture Challenges
questions: Why does a presentation culture become entrenched at my company?
How can we promote a writing culture without adding huge new documentation burdens?
What rituals can we modify to include short writing, review, and debrief?
How do we shift from transactional “write X get approval” documents to living, collaborative writing?
search_terms: how to get teams to write and give feedback
silent reading silent writing meeting ritual
summary: This post addresses how to build a writing culture in organizations by reframing writing as a reading-and-feedback practice that requires protected time for thinking. It contrasts writing cultures with presentation cultures driven by busyness, optics, power/control, and reactive tempo, and argues you can’t simply “switch” without changing those underlying conditions. It offers practical interventions—small writing components in existing rituals, scheduled write/review/debrief blocks, and iterative “living” documents—for leaders and teams who want more reflective, collaborative knowledge work.

title: TBM 53/52: "Are All Companies Messed Up?"
questions: Are all companies chaotic or “messed up,” or do they vary in how they’re affected?
How do I distinguish acute problems from chronic organizational issues?
What should leaders prioritize when a company is in survival mode?
How can teams address chronic issues without burning out?
summary: The post argues that companies differ widely in how “messed up” they are, depending on the mix of acute stressors and chronic, deep-rooted problems. It offers an ecosystem-style framework for understanding organizational health, emphasizing that sustainable productivity and resilience come from paying down chronic issues rather than only reacting to short-term fires. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners trying to make sense of persistent dysfunction, avoid burnout, and choose the right interventions.

title: TBM 44/52: Dissonance, Diffusion, and Debt
questions: What should you do when dissonance becomes unbearable at work?
How does dissonance get transferred from one role to another in an organization?
Why does organizational “debt” feel non-urgent to individuals even when it accumulates?
What does it mean to release “stored” dissonance during transformation?
summary: The post explains how cognitive dissonance in workplaces is managed through a set of coping mechanisms and how, when thresholds are exceeded, people shift burdens, degrade work, reframe narratives, or leave. It argues that dissonance diffuses across roles and systems, making organizational “debt” hard to perceive while still accumulating. It is aimed at leaders and teams planning change or transformation who need to understand what gets “released” beneath the surface.

title: TBM 40/52: Drivers vs. Fixers
questions: What should I do when I hit a roadblock: push forward pragmatically or fix the underlying mess?
How do incentives and rewards cause drivers and fixers to reinforce each other’s worst patterns?
Why do teams get stuck in exclusion and mono-culture between operators and thinkers?
How should leaders structure teams so drivers and fixers can partner effectively?
summary: This post contrasts two organizational mindsets—drivers who optimize for near-term forward progress and fixers who focus on untangling underlying problems. It argues that when one mindset dominates, self-reinforcing loops of exclusion, stereotypes, and mono-culture emerge, harming both progress and retention. The piece is aimed at leaders and team builders who need to design incentives and team structures that let drivers and fixers partner productively.

title: TBT 35/52: The Benefits of Stubborn Consistency
questions: What do we value, and do team members clearly understand what “good” looks like?
Are our practices culturally dependent, and do we apply them consistently enough to create predictability?
Are we opinionated and coherent—or incoherent and maladaptive?
If conditions change, will we update beliefs and try new ideas with enough curiosity?
summary: The post argues that team and organizational success depends less on any single engineering practice and more on coherence, intentionality, and consistent cultural signaling. It uses a pair-programming example to show how stubborn conviction can be effective when paired with fairness and the ability to update beliefs as conditions change, and it challenges leaders to examine what they value and how they model “good.”

title: TBM 27/52: When Is Change More Likely?
questions: What actually predicts whether an organizational change effort will lead to lasting behavior change?
How can leaders tell the difference between change theater and structural change?
Which system leverage points should we target to shift incentives, constraints, and information flows?
What kinds of interventions have the best odds of improving outcomes in corporate settings?
search_terms: where to intervene in a system for change
power dynamics and collaboration patterns in change
summary: The post argues that lasting change in organizations usually requires structural shifts—such as changes to priorities, team structures, incentives/punishments, dependencies, and constraints—rather than surface-level process updates and “talk.” It is aimed at leaders and managers who want to increase the odds that transformation efforts produce real behavioral change, using systems thinking and leverage points as a guide.

title: TBM 21/52: Old Guard, New Guard
questions: How do you bridge the gap between an old guard that understands the mess and a new guard that wants to avoid it?
What knowledge transfer practices help when implicit understanding doesn’t get communicated?
How can teams balance long-game novelty with quick wins and regularity?
Why do informal networks block new guard efforts, and how should leaders respond?
search_terms: quick wins vs long game strategy
summary: This post contrasts “old guard” and “new guard” mindsets in organizations facing persistent complexity. It’s for leaders and team members trying to manage transitions, improve knowledge transfer, and align trust, influence, and execution styles between experienced insiders and newly arrived newcomers.

title: 20 Things I've Learned as a Systems (Over) Thinker
questions: How do I manage burnout when my brain is working overtime?
How can I influence people when formal authority doesn’t capture real power dynamics?
What’s the best way to balance deep thinking with simplifying and communicating clearly?
How do I pursue long-term change without tying my identity too tightly to it?
summary: A practical, list-based set of lessons for people who tend to overthink and think in systems, focused on managing cognitive load and communicating effectively. It also addresses how to navigate influence, power, and change—emphasizing informal dynamics, collaboration, pacing, and realistic expectations about progress over time.

title: TBM 39/53:  Neither, Either, While, Without
questions: How do we preserve one priority while enabling another without harming our ability to do the second?
How do we course-correct after the pendulum swings too far in one direction without losing what matters?
How do we build a new ability when most inertia is focused elsewhere?
How do we scale an approach beyond pockets without creating new constraints or risks?
search_terms: workshop fill in the blanks prompts
summary: The post offers a set of fill-in-the-blank workshop prompts for leaders and facilitators to surface and discuss organizational polarities and tensions. It’s aimed at teams trying to make progress on competing priorities without falling into easy either-or answers. The prompts emphasize course correction, constraint removal, and scaling initiatives while managing unintended impacts.

title: TBM 33/53: But Why Is It Working?
questions: Why do different organizational cultures produce similarly positive outcomes for customers and teams?
How can I tell whether a practice like DRI, OKRs, or meeting size will fit my team’s culture?
Is psychological safety universal, or does it manifest differently across environments?
How do my biases and worldview affect which work ideas I adopt?
search_terms: why organizational practices work differently by culture
opposite of Anna Karenina principle in organizations
summary: The post argues that successful organizations may be culturally very different, so practices that work in one context can fail when copied into another. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners who want to choose tools and operating mechanisms (like DRI, OKRs, meeting norms, and psychological safety practices) based on cultural fit, supported by organizational self-awareness, bias recognition, and intentional listening.

title: TBM 22/53: Attacking X vs. Starting Y
questions: How do we stop success theater without escalating conflict?
What should we replace an 'optics and smoke and mirrors' narrative with?
Are we getting dragged down by what we are trying to stop?
Where can we focus on a better, healthier narrative that people will embrace?
search_terms: we go where we look attention
plant and grow a better narrative
summary: This post addresses how to respond to “success theater” in teams—performative celebrations and optics that feel shallow or misleading. It’s for leaders and change agents who want to reduce that behavior by redirecting attention and replacing it with a more authentic narrative that delivers momentum, recognition, and closure in a deeper way.

title: TBM 15/53: Why Can't This Be Like...
questions: Why can’t our team execute like a past “successful” project?
How do we deal with amnesia or selective memory about what enabled earlier wins?
What should leaders do when people are stuck in old narratives and blame stories?
How can we create a new narrative that restores urgency and delivery?
search_terms: why can't we replicate past success
summary: The post explores why teams often fail to recreate the conditions of a past “heroic” project, arguing that fixed narratives about earlier success create narrative inertia. It’s aimed at leaders and managers who need practical ways to restore execution by changing the environment (cadence, access, autonomy, and blocker removal) so a new, compelling story can emerge.
